coredata.dc:title: One-step green synthesis of oil-dispersible carbonized polymer dots as eco-friendly lubricant additives with superior dispersibility, lubricity, and durability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Yang, Qingbin
Peng, Jie
Yan, Ruixin
Zhao, Bin
Ge, Yicen
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Chemical functionalization provides effective protocols for inorganic nanomaterials as oil-dispersible lubricant additives. Nevertheless, harsh reaction conditions and arduous post-processing are frequently encountered when adopting this approach. Herein, four types of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with admirable dispersibility and long-term stability (more than 6 months without any sediments) in polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were synthesized by a one-step and green solvothermal route using saccharides as the single precursors. The tribological behaviors of CPDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200 were systematically evaluated and compared, confirming that the anti-wear and friction-reducing performances of PEG200 can be effectively enhanced after blended with CPDs. Clarified by the friction evaluations and worn surface detections, the superior lubricity and durability of CPDs-c are mainly attributed to the synergy between the interfacial adsorption of polymeric shells, the nano-lubrication effects of carbon cores, and the establishment of CPDs-inserted tribofilm with a uniform thickness of about 86 nm. This work explores a green and facile strategy for synthesizing the CPDs toward oil lubrication and reveals the lubrication mechanism of CPDs, which facilitates the practical application of CPDs in tribology.

head.ce:title: One-step green synthesis of oil-dispersible carbonized polymer dots as eco-friendly lubricant additives with superior dispersibility, lubricity, and durability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical functionalization provides effective protocols for inorganic nanomaterials as oil-dispersible lubricant additives. Nevertheless, harsh reaction conditions and arduous post-processing are frequently encountered when adopting this approach. Herein, four types of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with admirable dispersibility and long-term stability (more than 6 months without any sediments) in polyethylene glycol (PEG200) were synthesized by a one-step and green solvothermal route using saccharides as the single precursors. The tribological behaviors of CPDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200 were systematically evaluated and compared, confirming that the anti-wear and friction-reducing performances of PEG200 can be effectively enhanced after blended with CPDs. Clarified by the friction evaluations and worn surface detections, the superior lubricity and durability of CPDs-c are mainly attributed to the synergy between the interfacial adsorption of polymeric shells, the nano-lubrication effects of carbon cores, and the establishment of CPDs-inserted tribofilm with a uniform thickness of about 86 nm. This work explores a green and facile strategy for synthesizing the CPDs toward oil lubrication and reveals the lubrication mechanism of CPDs, which facilitates the practical application of CPDs in tribology.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, energy security and environmental sustainability are gradually evolving into a global consensus [1,2]. As one of the most mature and widely used energy-saving technologies, lubrication plays an indispensable role in improving energy utilization efficiency in mechanical systems [3,4]. Lubricants with outstanding tribological behaviors are urgently required to offer efficient lubrication for the active mechanical components under harsh environments, such as high temperature, heavy load, aggressive corrosion, extreme shearing stress, and so forth [5–7]. Unfortunately, conventional lubricants are insufficient to maintain the desired lubricity and durability for the steady operation of machinery. The proper introduction of additional additives with favorable anti-wear and friction-reducing abilities has been recognized as a feasible and dependable method to ameliorate the poor lubricity of base oils [8,9].

Carbonaceous nanostructures, including graphene/graphene oxide (GO) [10–12], fullerene [13–15], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [16–18], carbon nano-diamonds [19–21], and carbon nanoparticles [22–24] have recently shown attractive prospects as lubricant additives owing to their fascinating thermal stability, favorable chemical inertness, negligible corrosivity, stable mechanical and shearing strength, and remarkable self-lubricating and load-carrying capacity [25]. Typically, Om P. Khatri et al. confirmed the feasibility of ionic liquids (ILs) covalently attached graphene (GO-ILs) as the high-performance lubricant additives for PEG200, a class of eco-friendly and degradable synthetic base oil. In contrast with pure PEG200, the lubricant with 0.02 mg/mL of the hybrid GO-ILs demonstrated 55–78% and 14–23% reductions in average wear volume and friction coefficient (COF), respectively [12]. Fan et al. recently demonstrated that the amine/hydroxyl-terminated ILs modified GO can act as lubricant additives with outstanding dispersibility and lubricity, which further help to reveal the lubrication mechanism of 2D nanomaterials [26–27]. Zhou et al. reported the effectiveness of the imidazolium-based ILs grafted multi-walled CNTs (MWCNT-ILB) as the lubricant additives of 1-methyl-3-butylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate (L-B104) lubricant. The MWCNT-ILB efficiently enhanced the anti-wear and friction-reducing behaviors of L-B104 under various test parameters [18]. Notwithstanding these traditional carbon nanomaterials possess many tribological features as eco-friendly and efficient lubricant additives, the research on the region is highly restricted due to their unsatisfactory dispersibility and stability in base lubricants.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), a class of accidentally discovered and easily prepared carbon-based nanomaterials composed of polymeric shells and carbonaceous cores [28,29], have aroused increasing research attention in tribology owing to their novel and peculiar properties, such as small dimensions and copious groups with high interfacial reactivity [30–33]. Generally, the synthesis of CPDs could be summarized as two strategies, including “bottom-up” routes and “post-synthetic” modifications [34,35]. In terms of the traits of surface designability of carbon dots (CDs), Wang and coauthors proposed that the CPDs synthesized by grafting the poly (ionic liquid) s (PILs) and polyelectrolytes onto CDs could act as the high-efficiency anti-wear and friction-reducing additives of PEG200 [30–33]. In the above methodologies, many tedious and arduous surface modifications, such as atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), protonation, anion exchange, and phase transfer procedures, were used to overcome the problem closely related to the dispersibility and stability of CDs in base oils. Meanwhile, the employment of expensive chemicals, the emission of contaminants, and the low synthetic yield of CDs/CPDs remain inevitable issues [36–46]. Therefore, the cleaner production of the CPDs-based lubricant additives with satisfactory compatibility with base oils is urgently needed. Moreover, the underlying lubrication mechanism of CPDs also needs to be clarified.

In this study, four types of CPDs with various shell thicknesses and excellent dispersion stability in PEG200 were produced by one-step green solvothermal synthesis using saccharides as precursors. The physicochemical properties of CPDs were minutely disclosed. The tribological behavior of CPDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200 was systematically investigated by a linearly reciprocating high-speed friction tester installed with a steel/steel contact ball-on-disc apparatus. The influences of the dose on the tribological performances of CPDs-c were revealed and analyzed in detail. The service life of CPDs-c was further evaluated. The wear tracks on the worn steel surfaces were characterized detailly. In terms of the friction test results and surface characterizations, the synergistic lubrication mechanism of the polymeric shells and carbonaceous cores of CPDs was elucidated.

Anhydrous glucose (≥99.0%, RG grade), sucrose (≥99.0%, RG grade), dextran (RG grade, Mw ≈ 20.0 kg/mol), chitosan (RG grade, degree of deacetylation ≥ 90.0%), and polyethylene glycol (PEG200, Mw ≈ 200.0 g/mol, RG grade) were provided by Shanghai Adamas-Beta Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (China). Absolute ethanol (≥99.7%, AR grade) and hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 31.0 wt% in water) were supplied by Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (China). All reagents and chemicals were directly used as received.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, four types of CPDs were prepared by an H2O2-assisted solvothermal method we reported earlier [47]. Typically, glucose (1.0 g), ethanol (30 ml), and H2O2 (3 ml) were consecutively placed and encapsulated into a Teflon-inbuilt stain-less steel reaction vessel (100 ml). The reactor was heated to 200 °C for 12 h. After being naturally cooled to environment temperature, the resulting brownish-black solution was filtered and further centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to purge the visible precipitates and aggregates. Then a fuscous and viscous solid was obtained after recycling the ethanol with a rotary evaporator. The crude product was redispersed and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 4 days to eliminate the molecular impurities. Finally, a brown powder denoted as CPDs-g was collected by lyophilization. Similarly, the CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c were achieved with the above approach by substituting the glucose with sucrose, dextran, and chitosan, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1b, the underlying formation mechanism of CPDs-g can be attributed to the collaboration of oxidation, dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization. Based on the mass ratio of the CPDs and precursors, the production yields of CPDs-g, CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c are quantified as 18.6%, 15.1%, 17.2%, and 16.5%, respectively. It needs to be emphasized that all the as-prepared CPDs can be facilely redispersed into ethanol to form the pellucid dispersion without visible aggregates. Additionally, bright blue fluorescence can be emitted from the dispersion when being excited by UV light (365 nm) (Fig. S1), notarizing the remarkable dispersibility of CPDs in alcohols.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM2100) photographs were captured at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV to disclose the microstructural and morphological features of CPDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven BI-200SM) results were collected to characterize the hydrodynamic diameters of CPDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance X’ Pert PRO) patterns were recorded in the 2θ range from 5° to 80° to reveal the crystalline properties of CPDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, WQF-520) spectra were gained in the wavenumber range from 500 to 4000 cm−1 to unveil the chemical information of CPDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher 250XI) spectra were obtained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray radioactive source (1486.7 eV) to determine the elemental species and compositions of CPDs. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA449F3) curves were acquired from 30 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under an ultrapure Ar atmosphere to study the thermal decomposition behaviors of CPDs. The apparent viscosity of PEG200 and lubricants was measured by the HAAKE MARS III 600 rotational rheometer (HAAKE, Germany) in the temperature range of 25–50 °C with a heating rate of 2.5 °C/min and a shear rate of 7.34 s−1. The wettability of PEG200 and lubricants on the surface of fresh steel disc was determined with an SDC-200S contact angle meter (Dongguan Shengding Precision Instruments Co., LTD, China) at 25 °C. The Zeta-potentials of CPDs in ethanol are measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK).

The tribological behaviors of CPDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200 were investigated by an HSR-2M high-speed friction and wear tester equipped with a reciprocally sliding configuration. All the tribological tests were executed by squeezing down a fixed GCr15 steel ball (the radius of 3 mm, the Poisson's ratio of 0.3, the rigidity of about 60 HRC, and the elastic modulus of 208 GPa) against a reciprocally mobilizable GCr15 steel disc (the dimensions of Φ40 × 30 × 2 mm, and the Poisson's ratio of 0.3, the rigidity of about 60 HRC, and the elastic modulus of 208 GPa) under the default parameters: normal loads, 20–100 N (with an interval of 40 N); reciprocating speeds, 2–10 cm/s (with an interval of 2 cm/s); stroke, 5 mm; test duration, 30 and 300 min; ambient temperature and humidity. The Hertz contact stresses coinciding with the normal loads of 20, 60, and 100 N were respectively calculated as 1.78, 2.56, and 3.04 GPa according to the simplified Hertz’s formula:

, R0, E, and P stand for the Hertz contact stress, radius, elastic modulus, and normal load, respectively. About 0.5 ml of the formulated lubricant was injected into the steel/steel contact region by a medical syringe before testing. The real-time COF curves and average COF values of each measurement were in situ monitored and collected by a data-acquiring system connected to the tester furnished with a sensitive pressure sensor with a load-bearing capacity of 0–200 N. The tribological behaviors of each lubricant were evaluated at least four times to confirm reliability and repeatability. In terms of the white-light interferometry, the 2D and 3D profiles of the entire worn surfaces were facilely scanned and reconstructed by a 3D profilometer (CHOTEST, Super View W1) to collect wear volumes.

The worn surfaces were repeatedly cleaned with absolute ethanol to remove the residual wear debris and lubricants on the wear tracks before the following characterizations. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6710F, JEOL) was utilized to disclose the surface morphology of the wear tracks. The elemental species and distributions on the worn surfaces were probed by EDS. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to analyze the tribochemical products formed on the worn surfaces. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM2100F) linked with EDS was selected to observe the morphological and microstructural features of the tribofilm. A cross-sectional sample for HRTEM analysis was manufactured from the wear track on the worn steel substrate by using a focused ion beam system (FIB, Strata 400S, FEI).

As revealed by Fig. 2 a–2d, the TEM images indicate the generation of monodispersed and quasi-spherical CPDs without visible aggregates. The average particle sizes of CPDs-g, CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c are 3.65, 3.87, 3.53, and 3.42 nm, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 2e. It is worth noting that a myriad of amorphous particles representing the aggregation of polymeric precursors can be observed from Fig. 2c–2d, demonstrating that the CPDs-d and CPDs-c are the carbonized polymer dots composed of polymeric shells and carbonaceous cores, virtually. In contrast, a spot of amorphous structures can be found in Fig. 2a–2b, reflecting the higher degree of carbonization and the scarce surface organic moieties of CPDs-g and CPDs-s. As evidenced by HRTEM images, a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was observed in all the individual CPDs with a crystalline structure, confirming the good crystallinity of carbon cores. Some amorphous components corresponding to the polymeric shells can be identified from the surface of carbon cores, disclosing the formation of CPDs with a typical core–shell structure. The above viewpoints also can be further explained by the hydrodynamic diameters and size distributions of CPDs in ethanol determined by DLS results, as shown in Fig. 2f and Fig. S2.

As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, all the XRD patterns display a substantially uniform shape and noise, reflecting the similar crystallinity of CPDs. The CPDs-g exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 21.5° coinciding with the lattice distance of 0.21 nm, hinting at the lower crystallinity of CPDs-g [48]. It is widely recognized that the molecular structure of precursors is of vital importance to the production of CPDs with different properties [49]. These peaks gained by CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c were located at 21.3°, 21.2°, and 20.9°, respectively, confirming that their lattice distances are larger than CPDs-g, which can be well explained by the retention of polymeric shells containing organic groups with bigger hydraulic or atomic radius, such as hydroxy, carboxy, and amino groups, onto the surface of carbonaceous cores [33,50].

As depicted in Fig. 3b, several peaks are closely associated with the calibrated stretching vibration of OH (3425 cm−1), CH (2928 cm−1), CO (1710 cm−1), CC (1619 cm−1), and CO (1113 cm−1), bending vibration of CO (1270 cm−1), and deformation vibration of OH (1384 cm−1) can be observed distinctly form the FTIR spectra, demonstrating the existence of copious hydroxyl (–OH), carboxy (–COOH), methylene (–CH2–), methyl (–CH3), and ether (COC) groups onto CPDs [51]. Two typical absorption peaks closely related to the normative stretching vibration of NH (3374 cm−1) and OC–N (1651 cm−1) can be identified from the spectrum of CPDs-c, indicating that abundant nitrogen-containing groups were generated onto CPDs-c [52].

As disclosed by Fig. 3c, the CPDs-g, CPDs-s, and CPDs-d merely consist of C and O elements. Whereas, the higher fraction of N (5.8%) can be detected from the full-scan spectrum of CPDs-c, indicating the generation of nitrogen-containing groups onto CPDs-c. The fine C1s spectra of various CPDs displayed in Fig. S3 can be divided into three peaks located at approximately 288, 286, and 284 eV, which are allocated to CO, CO, and CC/CC, respectively [53]. Two signals appeared at approximately 533 and 532 eV in the O1s spectra of various CPDs can be designated to CO and CO binding energies, respectively [54]. Crucially, the high-resolution N1s spectrum of CPDs-c (Fig. S4) exhibits two species at about 400 and 402 eV, demonstrating that the N element is mostly in the form of pyrrolic and graphitic N [55,56]. The XPS results affirm the existence of copious organic moieties on these CPDs derived from saccharides.

As displayed in Fig. 3d, the apparent mass losses at 204°, 197°, 152°, and 171° can be identified as the initial decomposition of surface organic components of CPDs-g, CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c, respectively. In addition, the terminal mass fractions of CPDs-d (44.8%) and CPDs-c (44.2%) are lower than those of CPDs-g (49.3%) and CPDs-s (47.5%), directly confirming the lower proportion of carbon cores of CPDs-d and CPDs-c than CPDs-g and CPDs-s. In other words, the amorphous shells of CPDs-d and CPDs-c derived from polymeric precursors are thicker than those of CPDs-g and CPDs-s stemmed from macromolecular carbon sources. The above results testify that the CPDs with approximate core and various shell thicknesses were easily synthesized by the green H2O2-assisted solvothermal approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersibility, viscosity, and wettability of CPDs-based lubricants
ce:section.ce:para: As everyone knows, dispersion stability is crucial for lubricant additives to realize their full tribological potential [57–59]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the CPDs exhibit excellent dispersibility in PEG200 under ultrasonic treatment. Distinctly, no visible aggregates can be found from the formulated CPDs (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants under moderate (setting at 25 °C for 6 months) and rigorous conditions (freezing at –20 °C for a month, centrifuging at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, and heating at 150 °C for a week, respectively). Moreover, all the lubricants seem like the clear and pellucid solutions under sunlight and emit the bright yellow fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) (Fig. S5), confirming the outstanding dispersibility of CPDs in PEG200.

It is widely believed that the viscosity and wettability of lubricants are of vital importance to their tribological properties. As shown in Fig. 4b, all the apparent viscosities of pure PEG200 and lubricants gradually decrease with the increasing temperature, which is attributed to the reduced intermolecular and intramolecular forces of PEG. All the lubricants show a slightly lower viscosity than PEG200 in the range of 25–50 °C, indicating that the interaction between PEG molecules is barely affected by the dispersed CPDs. That is, the effect of CPDs on the viscosity of PEG200 is negligible. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the contact angles of lubricants are smaller than that of PEG200, indicating the wettability of PEG200 on the steel surface can be improved by CPDs. Thus, the good wettability of lubricants on the steel surface directly led to the outstanding lubricity of CPDs.

As generally recognized, the physicochemical and tribological properties of CPDs are closely associated with the molecular structure of precursors that should be carefully designed and chosen [60]. To disclose the structure–function relationships between the type of saccharides and their derived CPDs, the anti-wear and friction-reducing behaviors of various CPDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200 were evaluated in detail. Fig. 5 a presents the smoothed COF curves of PEG200 and various CPDs (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants as a function of test duration at the normal load of 20 N. A distinct running-in period before 8 min and a maximal COF value of approximate 0.17 can be seen from the COF curve with bigger fluctuations gained by PEG200. As expected, all the relatively stable COF curves achieved by various lubricants display the downward trend at the beginning of the friction experiment and remain at a relatively lower level throughout the entire process, implying the outstanding tribological behaviors of CPDs. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the most significant reductions of 33.2% and 35.1% in the mean wear volume and COF for PEG200 can be obtained by introducing CPDs-c, intuitively reflecting the optimal anti-wear and friction-reducing function of CPDs-c among the four kinds of the CPDs-based additives. In comparison, the mean wear volume and COF reductions for PEG200 are merely 10.3% and 15.7%, 21.6% and 21.0%, and 30.8% and 28.7%, respectively, when the same content of CPDs-g, CPDs-s, and CPDs-d are blended. Besides, the lubricity of the polysaccharides-derived CPDs-d and CPDs-c are noticeably higher than those of the mono-/di-saccharides-derived CPDs-g and CPDs-s, adequately demonstrating that the tribological behaviors of CPDs are closely related to the chemical structure of precursors.

Fig. 6 exhibits the 2D, 3D images and cross-sectional profiles of wear tracks lubricated with neat PEG200 and CPDs (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants. The largest depth (9.41 μm) and width (476.17 μm) can be observed from the wear track lubricated by PEG200 with ordinary lubricity. In contrast with PEG200, the depth and width of the wear tracks lubricated with CPDs-g (0.10 wt%)/, CPDs-s (0.10 wt%)/, CPDs-d (0.10 wt%)/, and CPDs-c (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants maximally decrease to 8.39 and 460.54 μm, 7.65 and 446.91 μm, 7.14 and 432.28 μm, and 6.30 and 402.84 μm, respectively. Additionally, all the cross-sectional areas of the wear tracks achieved by lubricants are smaller than that of PEG200, reflecting the superior anti-wear function of CPDs. The CPDs-c exhibits the best tribological properties due to the superior interfacial adsorption of the nitrogen-enriching groups onto CPDs-c, which plays a crucial part in dragging the carbonaceous cores of CPDs-c into the friction region for friction-reducing and anti-wear. To disclose the interfacial adsorption difference of CPDs, the Zeta-potential measurement for the CPDs/ethanol dispersions was performed. As shown in Fig. S6, the mean Zeta-potentials of CPDs-g, CPDs-s, and CPD-d are −6.2, −7.6, and −11.5 mV, respectively, hinting that the negatively charged CPDs capped with abundant oxygen-containing groups are easily absorbed into the friction region by electrostatic adsorption to act as “ball-bearings” for friction-reducing and load-carrying. However, the CPDs-c with a Zeta-potential of 19.1 mV exhibits the best tribological properties, profited by the outstanding coordination and complexation between the Fe2+/Fe3+ on the friction surface and the nitrogen-containing groups onto CPDs, such as amino and amide. These complex interactions should be responsible for the higher interfacial adsorption ability and tribological performance of CPDs-c.

To further evaluate the tribological potential of various CPDs under high Hertz contact pressure, the tribological properties of PEG200 and CDs (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants were measured and compared at the normal loads of 60 and 100 N, respectively. As evidenced by Fig. S7a–S8a, all the COF curves obtained by lubricants are lower than that of PEG200, confirming the superb lubricity of CPDs as micro “ball-bearings” that provides the polishing, rolling, and mending function to alleviate the wear and friction. The identical regularity in the order of friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of CPDs can be observed from the mean wear volumes and COFs shown in Fig. S7b–S8b, adequately reflecting the steady load-carrying capacity of CPDs at a wide range of normal loads. When the CPDs-c is employed as lubricant additives at loads of 60 and 100 N, the average wear volume and COF reductions for PEG200 are 37.7% and 31.0%, and 41.5% and 32.5%, respectively, confirming that the CPDs-c is the optimal modifier. The conclusions coinciding with previous results can be drawn in terms of the cross-sectional profiles and corresponding 2D and 3D images of wear tracks exhibited in Fig. S9–S10. It is well proved that the polymeric shells and N elements onto CPDs-c played a vital part in enhancing the tribological behaviors of PEG200.

The concentration is of vital importance in regulating the lubricity of lubricant additives [61]. Fig. 7 a gives the COF curves obtained by lubricants with the increasing dosage of CPDs-c at the load of 20 N. As expected, all the COF curves gained by the lubricants are observably smoother and lower than PEG200, proving the remarkable friction-reducing function of CPDs-c. The lubricant containing 0.12 wt% of CPDs-c acquires the lowest COF curve with a continuous downtrend, determining the most appropriate dosage of CPDs-c. Fig. 7b further confirms the most relevant content of CPDs-c to be 0.12 wt%, as verified by the reductions of 50.7% and 47.6% in the average wear volume and COF of PEG200, respectively. Moreover, the deteriorative friction-reducing and anti-wear behaviors can be found when the excess CPDs-c was added, directly illustrating the commonality of the abrasive wear induced by the aggregation of superfluous nano-additives. The above tribological results testify that the CPDs-c as the lubricant additives of PEG200 offers favorable anti-wear and friction-reducing performances, especially at the most suitable dose of 0.12 wt%.

One of the considerable parameters to determine the quality of lubricant additives is their service life [62,63]. To evaluate the lifetime of CPDs-c, the friction duration was extended tenfold from 30 to 300 min. As depicted in Fig. 7c, the COF curve originating from PEG200 rises to about 0.17 after four repeating “down-up” stages, demonstrating its ordinary and unstable friction-reducing ability. Compared with PEG200, the COF curve stemmed from the CPDs-c (0.12 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant declines steadily after a running-in stage (0–30 min) and ascends tardily after 200 min, reflecting the relatively steady friction-reducing property of CPDs-c. As shown in Fig. 7d, the mean wear volume and COF obtained by the lubricant are 50.3% and 35.2% less than that of PEG200, respectively, indicating the tribological properties of CPDs-c did not deteriorate dramatically under the prolonged test duration. It is generally believed that the lubricity and durability of nanoparticles as lubricant additives are closely related to their nano-lubrication function as “third-particles” and the formation of the particles-inserted tribofilm. The above viewpoints can be further agreed upon by the cross-sectional profiles and 2D, 3D images of wear tracks displayed in Fig. S11–S12.

Numerous studies have notarized that the organic/inorganic hybrids and composites as lubricant additives could transform into tribofilm by interfacial adsorption and tribochemical reactions [12,57]. To gain insight into the potential lubrication mechanism of CPDs-c as lubricant additives of PEG200, SEM-EDS was conducted to detect the elemental species and surface morphologies of the worn surfaces lubricated by PEG200 and CPDs-c (0.12 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant, respectively. As presented in Fig. 8 a, a myriad of scratches, furrows, and grooves can be easily discerned from the worn surface gained by PEG200, implying its inferior anti-wear and friction-reducing performances. By contrast, scarce scratches, furrows and grooves, an obscure black layer representing tribofilm, lower fractions of Fe and O, and higher proportions of C and N were observed from the worn surface lubricated with lubricant (Fig. 8b), affirming the deposition of CPDs-c on the rubbed surface, which accounts for the superb tribological behaviors of CPDs-c.

As presented in Fig. 9 , the fine XPS spectra were employed to analyze the elemental species of friction products that emerged on the worn surfaces lubricated with CPDs-c (0.12 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant and pure PEG200, respectively. These peaks appear at about 706.6, 710.2, 713.1, 723.1, and 725.8 eV can be allocated to the normative binding energies of metallic Fe, Fe2p3/2 (FeO), Fe2p3/2 (Fe2O3), Fe2p1/2 (Fe2O3), and Fe2p1/2 (FeO), indicating that the dominating forms of Fe on the worn surface lubricated with PEG200 are the metallic and oxidized iron, which also can be confirmed collectively by the binding energy at 529.8 eV in the spectrum of O1s [57,58]. In sharp contrast, the weak binding energy of the Fe2p3/2 satellite at 718.1 eV, two intense signals of CO/CN (286.2 eV) and CO (288.1 eV), the higher proportions of CO (530.9 eV) and CO (532.2 eV), and the distinct peaks of graphitic N (400.1 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.3 eV) can be respectively detected from the Fe2p, C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra of the worn surface achieved by the lubricant, proving that the CPDs-c was deposited on the friction interfaces and took part in the construction of tribofilm. However, no convictive evidence for tribochemical reactions between the organic moieties of CPDs-c and metallic substrate can be collected from these XPS spectra and the elemental compositions summarized in Table S1, suggesting that the CPDs-c mainly acted as the “ball-bearings” and “nanofillers” during the friction process.

As shown in Fig. 10 , the cross-sectional TEM images of the wear track on the worn steel disc were acquired to uncover the structural and morphological features of the tribofilm deposited on the worn surface lubricated with CPDs-c (0.12 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant. Distinctly, a compact tribofilm with a uniform thickness of about 86 nm was generated on the surface of the steel substrate (Fig. 10a). A myriad of cohesive nanoparticles with an apparent lattice distance of 0.216 nm (lattice plane: 002) can be observed from the tribofilm matrix (Fig. 10b), clearly confirming the formation of the CPDs-c-inserted tribofilm, which was further clarified by the fast Fourier transform (FFT) analyses (Fig. 10c) for the cross-section including tribofilm matrix and iron oxide layer (Fe2O3, lattice distance of 0.208 nm, lattice planes of 004 and 111) [64]. In addition, many amorphous components identified as organic/iron composites and hybrids can be found in the tribofilm matrix, suggesting that the tribofilm is composed of CPDs-c and friction products, which can be further confirmed by the higher proportion of C, O, and N and the lower fraction of Fe obtained from EDS elemental mappings (Fig. 10d) of the protective tribofilm.

In terms of the friction test results and worn surface characterizations, the underlying lubrication mechanism of CPDs-c is proposed and elucidated in Fig. 11 . During the running-in stage, low-energy electrons are inclined to emit from the micro-convex of the fresh friction region, directly inducing the exposure of iron ions on the friction interfaces, i.e., the rubbing surfaces to be activated [36,56]. Then the copious organic groups onto CPDs-c were easily anchored to the activated interfaces by interfacial adsorptions, which plays a crucial part in dragging the carbonaceous cores of CPDs-c into the friction region. Further, the rigid carbon cores as rolling “ball-bearings” can exert the outstanding nanoscale lubrication function to polish the rough surfaces, mend the furrows and repair the damaged interfaces. Meanwhile, the polymeric shells of CPDs-c can be anchored in the metallic surfaces under mechanical and thermal effects, facilitating the deposition of CPDs-c on the rubbed surfaces and the generation of the protective tribofilm. Owing to the thicker polymeric shells with copious adsorption sites, the CPDs-d and CPDs-c showed better tribological performances than CPDs-g and CPDs-s with relatively thinner shells, which can be illustrated by the tribological investigations for different CPDs. The best tribological behaviors of CPDs-c can be attributed to its nitrogen-enriched organic groups with higher interfacial adsorption capacity. Worn surface characterizations confirmed the formation of the thicker tribofilm composed of the CPDs-c and iron compounds, hinting that friction products and carbon cores as “nanofillers” can be consecutively inserted into the tribofilm, consolidating the tribofilm matrix and leading to the largest friction and wear reductions of CPDs-c. It is generally believed that the good tribological behaviors of spherical nanoparticles as “third body” mainly depend on their nano-lubrication function and the formation of the protective tribofilm [65–66]. That is, the synergistic effects of the polymeric shells and carbon cores should be responsible for the remarkable anti-wear and friction-reducing behaviors of CPDs-c.

In summary, we employed a one-step H2O2-assisted solvothermal approach for the cleaner production of CPDs from saccharides. Four kinds of CPDs expressed as CPDs-g, CPDs-s, CPDs-d, and CPDs-c were facilely obtained from glucose, sucrose, dextran, and chitosan, respectively. A series of evidence gained by HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, XPS, and TGA indicated that the precursors with polymeric structures enable more residual groups onto CPDs. It was confirmed that copious organic segments with higher interfacial adsorption and reaction activity formed onto the CPDs-c. The outstanding dispersion stability of CPDs can be explained by the favorable affinity between PEG200 molecules and the surface groups of CPDs. It was indicated that the tribological performances of PEG200 can be effectively enhanced by merely being blended with CPDs. Compared with PEG200, the lubricant with an optimal dosage of 0.12 wt% of CPDs-c exhibited 50.7% and 47.6% less in the mean wear volume and COF, respectively. Clarified by the friction test results and worn surface characterizations (including FESEM-EDS, XPS, and FIB-TEM), the admirable anti-wear and friction-reducing ability, outstanding load-carrying capacity, and prolonged lifetime of CPDs-c were chiefly attributed to the synergistic effects between the interfacial adsorption of polymeric shells, the nanoscale lubrication of carbonaceous cores, and the formation of the CPDs-c-inserted tribofilm.
